content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Comrnission
July 11, 1990
John E. Golden
city clerk
100 Second Street
P. O. Box 716
Isleton, CA 95641
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. A 90-393
Dear Mr. Go1den:
You are seeking advice regarding use of funds under the use-
of-campaiqn-funds provisions of the Politica1 Reform Act (the
ItActtr).1 -tn" folllwing advice is based upon the facts provided in
your letter of May 29, L99O, and our subsequent telephone
conversatj-on of June L2, ]-990.
Although your letter notes that loca1 council'rnembers'
campaj.gn funds L\ril-L be affected by this advice, thj's letter onl-y
addiesies your particular situation because the councilmenbers'
questions Lave not been presented. The advice in this letter
should not be construed to cover their circumstances because
factual differences may change the result.
OUESTTON
(1) If a candi-date Loans noney to his canpaign account and
wins the election, may the loan be repaid fron the carnpaign ac-
count?
(2) How may an elected official 
use funds renaining in his
campaign bank account after the election?
CONCLUSTON
(1) Because the Po]itical Reform Act does not prohibit
repayment of personal loans nade to a canpaign account, the loan
rflay be repaid from the carnpaign account.
1 cor".rrr"nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government Code unl-ess otherwise indicated. 
Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 Californj,a Code of Regulations Section
18O0O, et se(I. A11 references to regulations are to Titl,e 2,
Divj.sion 5 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8OZ . Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) 3 22-5660


File No. A-90-393
Page 2
(2) Expenditures from a
only if reasonably related to
governmental purpose.
canrpaign bank account may be made
a political, 
legislative, 
or
re
As part of your campaign for city clerk this year, you opened
a checking account trith $100 of your personal, funds. The bank
required S1OO in order to open a checking account. Your sole
expendj-ture was $3.75 for copying expenses, and you won the elec-
tion. 
on June !2, 1990, you inforrued me by telephone that you
belj-eve you reported the 9100 as your personal loan to the
campaign account.
ANALYSTS
The Commission does not nake findings of fact, and therefore
cannot decide $/hether the funds in the checking account are
in fact a 1oan. This }etter first analyzes the situation based on
an assurnption that there was a personal loan, and then based on an
assurnption that the money was given without a prornise to repay.
(1) Loan
Nothing in the PoLitical Reform Act prohibits candidates from
accepting loans subject to repa)rnent. section 84216 provides that
a loin received by a candidate is a contribution and should be
reported. By setting dollar limits on camPaign Ioans, the Act
recognizes that candidates do accept 1oans.
Section 85201 requires that all contributions and loans made
to the candidate shall be deposited j.n a bank account. (Lazarus
Advice Letter, No. I-89-630, copy enclosed.)
The Act also regulates how campaign funds may be used. Pursu-
ant to section 85202, all contributions deposited into the
campaj-gn account |tshall- be deemed to be held in trust for expenses
associated with the election of the candidate ... or expenses as-
sociated with holding that of f ice. rt Because repayment of a loan
is an expense assocj.ated with the election, it is therefore a
perrnissible use of campaign funds under the trust provision of
85202. (Cov1e Advice Letter, No. A-89-538, copy enclosed.)
The Act also requires that the expenditure of campaign funds
be reasonably related to a political, 
governnental or legislative
purpose.z (Section 85801.) A loan received by a candidate or
)- If the expenditure results in a substantial personal benefit it
nust be directlv related to a political, 
legislative, 
or
governmental purpose. (Section 85801.) A substantial personal
benefit is one which results in a direct personal benefit of more
than $100 to a candidate or elected officer. 
(Section
85800(b) (2).) 
Since the anount at issue here is less than g1OO,
the expendi-ture need onry meet the resser standard of reasonabry
related. 
(Section 85801, )


Fi]'e No. A-90-393
Page 3
committee is a contribution unless the loan is received from a
commercial lending institution in the ordinary course of business,
or it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not
made for political purposes. (Section 842L6. ) The loan you rnade
to your carnpaign was clearLy for political 
purposes, and accord-
ingty the repayment of the loan is reasonabLy related to a
political 
purpose pursuant to section 85801.
rn conclusion, if you provided the S100 as a personal, loan
for your campaj.gn, the remaining funds in your campaign funds may
be used to repay the debt.
(2) contribution
If the $1oo was a contribution, the funds may be used only
for expenditures reasonably related to a political, 
J,egislative or
governmental purpose. (Section 85801.) Other sections specify
$rhat expenditures are permitted, but they are not intended to set
forth the only purposes for $rhich campaign contributions nay be
spent. (Sectioir SSaoz.l3 Any expenditure meeting the rrreasonably
related to a polj.tical or governmenta] purpose'r standard v/oul-d be
pernissible under the Act. As explained above, we believe that,
under the facts presented, the $L00 was expended for purposes that
!/ere reasonably related to a political, 
legislative or
governmental purpose.
3 For example, an election victory celebration or si.rnil-ar
carupaign event, or gifts totaling less than $100 in a cal-endar
year made to an employee or a carnpaign commi-ttee worker, or to an
employee of the el-ected officer's 
agency are considered to be
directly related to a political., legi,slatJ,ve, or governmental
purpose. (Section 85802 (f) (2) . )
As you noted in your letter, 
carnpaign funds may be used to
nake donations to bona fide charitabLe, educati,onaL, civi.c,
religious, or similar tax-exempt, nonprofit organizations. Sec-
tion 85803.
Carnpaign funds can be used to reimburse a candidate or
elected officer for use of his or her own vehicle if such use is
di,rectly related to a political, 
governrnental, or legj,slative
purpose. Sectj.on 85804 (d) .
Carnpaign funds may be used to compensate a candidate or
elected officer for out-of-pocket expenses incurred for political,
legislative, or governmental purposes (Section 85806), provided
that the special requirements of regulation 1852G are mLt.
As noted above, these provisions sinply serve as a guide to
help define the 1egal standard, rrreasonably related to a political
purpose.rr Other expenditures meeting this standard would also be
permissible.


File No. A-90-393
Page 4
I trust this Letter has provided you with the guj-dance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this mat-
ter please contact ne at (915) 322-590L.
sincerely,
scott Hallabrin
Pegg'y Bernardy J
counsel, Legal Division
SH: PB: plh
Enc Iosures
